title: Armenia’s election: Voters to decide on Pashinyan’s peace agenda
gist: Armenia's 7 June election is effectively a preliminary referendum on Pashinyan's 'Real Armenia' peace deal with Azerbaijan and his pivot to the West, set against deep domestic fractures over the loss of Karabakh and Armenia's enduring economic dependence on Russia.

themes.theme: Election as referendum on the peace deal

insights.claim: Incumbent PM Nikol Pashinyan and his Civil Contract party are campaigning under a 'Real Armenia' banner (renouncing wider territorial ambitions) for a final peace accord with Azerbaijan that would end four decades of conflict and integrate Armenia into regional connectivity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A Pashinyan win would clear the path for finalizing the peace deal; a loss or weak mandate would put the Azerbaijan settlement and regional connectivity plans at risk.
insights.locator: Under the banner of a ‘Real Armenia’

insights.claim: Signing the final peace agreement is contingent on Armenia adopting a new constitution — at Azerbaijan's insistence — that removes all references to Mountainous Karabakh, which will require a separate referendum after the election, making the 7 June vote effectively a preliminary referendum on Pashinyan's negotiated terms.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Watch for a second referendum on the constitution post-election as the actual binding step; the June election result will set the political mandate but not finalize the deal.
insights.locator: That makes this election effectively a preliminary referendum

insights.claim: At an August 2025 White House summit, Armenian and Azerbaijani foreign ministers initialled but did not sign a peace agreement in Trump's presence; there have been no military fatalities since February 2024, and Azerbaijan and Turkey have both taken steps to dismantle their long-standing blockade of Armenia.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Tangible de-blockading steps by Turkey and Azerbaijan are a leading indicator of real economic opening for landlocked Armenia if the peace process holds.

insights.claim: Armenia's definitive loss of Mountainous Karabakh to Azerbaijan in 2023 deprived Armenian nationalism of a founding pillar but also loosened Russian control over Armenian foreign policy, exposing Moscow's declining power in the South Caucasus and the limits of its patronage.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A key geopolitical loss paradoxically freed Armenia to realign externally — a pattern worth watching in other Russian-patronage states under strain.
insights.locator: Yet the loss of Karabakh has also loosened Russian control

insights.claim: The 2018 'Velvet Revolution' that brought Pashinyan to power deliberately left Armenia's Russia alliance intact, but the final 2023 loss of Karabakh released him from the need to uphold that alignment while simultaneously convincing the opposition that rapprochement with Moscow is the only way to avoid further calamity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Explains the ideological split defining this election: Civil Contract's Western pivot versus opposition blocs seeking Russia rapprochement.

insights.claim: Polls put Civil Contract ahead of the 'Strong Armenia' bloc led by Russian-Armenian billionaire Samvel Karapetyan with a plurality of voters onside, and a mid-May poll found 45 per cent of undecided voters believed Armenia was moving in the right direction; Pashinyan's progress is unlikely to be halted despite fears of foreign information manipulation.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Base case is a Pashinyan/Civil Contract election win, making the peace process and Western pivot the likely trajectory absent a polling surprise.
insights.locator: Polls put Civil Contract ahead of its nearest rival

insights.claim: The campaign has featured vitriolic exchanges between Pashinyan and citizens challenging his peace narrative and a circulating video of masked men threatening violence against him, alarming in a country that has seen repeated political violence including the 1999 assassination of an entire tier of parliamentary leadership.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Rising political violence risk around the peace process is a tail risk for post-election stability and implementation of any Azerbaijan settlement.
insights.locator: Such threats are not taken lightly

insights.claim: In a heated campaign-trail exchange, Pashinyan asked a Karabakh Armenian refugee why he was still alive, implying he should have stayed and died in Karabakh; the man was later arrested on a hooliganism charge, feeding accusations from civil society that government rhetoric enforces 'amnesia' about the Karabakh loss and displacement, and at times spills into hate speech toward former Karabakh Armenians.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Unresolved treatment of displaced Karabakh Armenians is a latent political liability that could resurface regardless of the election outcome or peace deal's formal terms.
insights.locator: For example, in a heated exchange on the campaign trail

themes.theme: Pivot to the West and multipolar hedging

insights.claim: The US has become a key peace broker through TRIPP (the Trump Route for International Peace and Prosperity), a planned trade, communications and energy transit route across southern Armenia linking Azerbaijan to its Nakhchivan exclave; Armenia also hosted the first-ever EU-Armenia summit alongside a European Political Community summit shortly before the campaign began.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: TRIPP is a concrete US-brokered infrastructure project to track as a proxy for how far the US-Armenia relationship and regional connectivity actually progress post-election.
insights.locator: Notably, the US has become a key peace broker

insights.claim: The more Russia perceives Armenia as a liability in a poaching contest with the EU, the more it will commit to that contest, which is risky given Armenia's significant dependencies on Russia for energy, food supply and remittances; conversely, the more Europe sees Armenia as vulnerable to Russian pressure, the easier it will be to overlook shortcomings in Pashinyan's democratic record.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Geopolitical competition for Armenia may reduce external scrutiny of Armenia's democratic backsliding risks — a governance risk investors in the region should track independently of the headline peace narrative.
insights.quote: The more that Europe sees Armenia as vulnerable to Russian pressure, the easier it will be to overlook shortcomings in Pashinyan’s democratic record.
insights.locator: Yet ‘pivots to the West’ also carry risks

insights.claim: All three South Caucasus states are converging on 'omni-alignment,' seeking to become nodes in wider Eurasian connectivity rather than choosing between Russia and the West; Armenia has been upgrading ties with Gulf states, South Asia and China, while its CSTO membership is a 'dead letter' but its Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU) membership still affords real benefits and Russia remains its single largest export market.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A binary Russia-vs-West investment or policy framework misreads Armenia; expect continued multi-vector trade and diplomatic ties even as Western political alignment deepens.
insights.locator: A choice between Russia and the West is also a reductive way
